title: Counting Calves Affected by Drought
subject: English Literacy / Mathematical Word Problem
extracted_text:
The lengths we go to keep our herd alive during the drought
By Hannah Brown, Prairie, QLD

I wonder whether the majority of Australians really understand what affect a drought has on the land, cattle industry and the cattle owners.

Waking up just before dawn, we pull on our boots and off we go without a word.

While Dad and my brother load bales of hay on the truck and tonnes of grain and hay mix into the trailer, Mum and I feed all the poddies.

First we feed the nursery, it has four baby heifers (they are fed homemade colostrum) and three calves that didn’t take to milk and need to be picked up every time we walk past.

They have little hope of survival.

The nursery poddies get four feeds a day.

After the first round is done, we head over to the toddlers.

These 38 calves are at least three weeks old.

Twelve take to milk and are fed three times a day, also getting hay, poddy pellets and molasses.

Then it is off to feed the 106 kindy and the 174 preschool calves that get hay, molasses and grain twice a day.

325 in total. It will be fewer come morning.

Some days are better than others.

The best days are when you don’t have to take away a poddy that’s died overnight.

Or the mornings you didn’t have to shoot one (it’s the humane thing to do).

On those horrid mornings, I slowly walk to the four-wheeler, tears rolling down my face.

I always make sure no one sees my tears. I don’t know why, I just don’t.

I tear myself apart over what I should have done better.

But that never changes the fact that the poor little bugger is gone... and it never stops the next one from going either.

I do everything in my power to help them, it just isn’t enough.

After the poddies are fed, we jump into the truck to feed the cattle that have been jammed into four paddocks around the house.

Once healthy beasts now have to be shot and dragged away.

Months passed without a drop of rain, so the heartbreaking decision to truck 90 per cent of the breeders off to be slaughtered was shattering.

We had put so much effort in to keeping them alive. For them to be killed was heartrending.

I am 18 and this was my day for months.

The lengths we go to keep our herd alive during the drought
By Hannah Brown, Prairie, QLD

I wonder whether the majority of Australians really understand what affect a drought has on the land, cattle industry and the cattle owners.

These 38 calves are at least three weeks old.

After the poddies are fed, we jump into the truck to feed the cattle that have been jammed into four paddocks around the house.

We had put so much effort in to keeping them alive. For them to be killed was heartrending.

I am 18 and this was my day for months.

How many calves are there in total?
A   38
B   106
C   174
D   325
explanation: The total number of calves mentioned in the passage is calculated by adding the number of calves in different categories stated: 38 toddlers, 106 kindy, and 174 preschool calves. Together they sum to 318. However, the passage explicitly states that the total cows (including unknown categories or possibly overall count) adds up to 325. Therefore, the correct answer is D, the overall total provided in the text is indeed 325.
